topic: Cantonese practice with Nai Nai — funny mistakes

turns.content: i just absolutely humiliated myself trying to speak cantonese and i need to tell someone about it

ok so i've been trying to practice my cantonese more with my nai nai right. like we always talk in cantonese and i can understand most of what she says but speaking it back is... a different story. so today i was trying to tell her that i was full after lunch
I feel like this is going somewhere good. What did you say instead?

i THOUGHT i said 'i'm full' but apparently the tone was wrong and what i actually said was closer to 'i'm pregnant.' nai nai just stared at me for a solid three seconds and then started yelling at my mum in the other room
Oh no. The tones got you. That must have caused some chaos before you could explain. How long did it take to clear up?

like a full minute of my mum running in looking panicked and nai nai gesturing at me and me standing there going 'no no no FULL. full from food. FOOD.' meanwhile my dad is in the kitchen absolutely losing it laughing. genuinely the worst thirty seconds of my life followed by the funniest thirty seconds of my life
Your dad laughing in the background while your mum and Nai Nai are in crisis mode is such a perfect family scene. Did Nai Nai find it funny once she understood?

eventually lol. she did the thing where she pretends to be annoyed but you can see her trying not to smile. then she spent the next twenty minutes drilling me on the correct tones for like ten different words. she takes the cantonese lessons VERY seriously
Honestly that's kind of amazing — an impromptu pronunciation lesson. Tonal languages are genuinely difficult if you didn't grow up speaking them regularly. Do you practice often with her?

i'm trying to do it more. like i understand heaps because i grew up hearing it at home and at the restaurant but actually producing the right tones is so hard. english has trained my mouth wrong. and cantonese has like six tones?? who decided that was necessary. every sentence is a minefield

Six tones is a lot to juggle, especially when you understand the language by ear but didn't grow up actively speaking it. The receptive vs productive gap is really common in heritage language speakers. Any other memorable mix-ups?

oh god yes. last week i tried to say 'the weather is hot' and i somehow said something about a horse?? i don't even know how. nai nai just looked at me with such disappointment lol. and one time i mixed up the word for 'book' and 'sleep' which made a sentence very confusing. she's patient though. mostly. she does sigh a lot
The patient sighing of a grandmother who loves you but cannot believe you just said that. That's universal. It sounds like she enjoys the teaching though, even with the sighs.

she does i think. like i reckon she's genuinely happy i'm trying. a lot of second gen kids don't bother and she knows that. i just want to be able to have actual conversations with her beyond the basics you know? she's got so many stories and i can only catch like 60% of them

That's a really meaningful reason to keep at it. The stories you're missing are probably incredible, and the fact that you're putting in the effort will mean a lot to her regardless of the tone mishaps along the way.

yeah... ok i'm getting emotional about it now lol. anyway the moral of the story is don't tell your cantonese grandmother you're pregnant when you mean you're full. noted. lesson learned. my mum's heart rate is probably still elevated
Your mum deserves some kind of compensation for that scare. Keep practicing though — the embarrassing mistakes are honestly the ones you never forget, so that tone is probably locked in for life now.
